areaTag.en: Family law · slip rule · evidence · costs

one.zh: 笔误更正规则（slip rule）——更正一项按固定金额分割「受控资金账户」、却遗漏了其后所生利息的终局财产令，使其符合法院 62.5/37.5 的本意；并就费用准予使用调解中编制的资产负债表。
one.en: Slip rule — correcting a final property order that split a Controlled Money Account by fixed amounts but omitted the interest it later accrued, to reflect the court's intended 62.5/37.5 division; and on costs, allowing use of a balance sheet prepared at mediation.

issue.zh: 法院能否依「笔误更正规则」（规则 10.13(e)——命令不反映法院本意）更正终局财产令，使妻获得受控资金账户所生利息的应得份额？调解中共同编制的资产负债表能否就费用问题举证？应否判夫承担妻的费用？
issue.en: Can the court use the 'slip rule' (r 10.13(e) — order not reflecting the court's intention) to correct the final orders so the wife receives her share of interest on a Controlled Money Account? Can a balance sheet jointly prepared at mediation be adduced on costs? And should the husband pay the wife's costs?

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – SLIP RULE – Where the final property orders did not make provision for the wife to receive interest accrued on monies held in a Controlled Money Account – Where the orders as pronounced did not reflect the intention of the presiding judicial officer – Where the order is set aside and replaced. FAMILY LAW – EVIDENCE – Mediation agreement provided for confidentiality – Whether a Balance Sheet jointly prepared during a mediation can be adduced on questions of costs. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Consideration of s 114UB – Where the Court is satisfied there are justifying circumstances to make an order for costs.

subseq.zh: 本案为同案系列第 2 号判决（实体终局判决见 Niek & Oddrun [2025] FedCFamC1F 802）。本资料库未见进一步记录。
subseq.en: This is the second decision in the series (the substantive final judgment is Niek & Oddrun [2025] FedCFamC1F 802). No further record in this library.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
本案是同一桩财产纠纷的「续集」。Anderson 法官于 2025-11-12 作出终局财产令：综合贡献（妻占 57.5/42.5）与 s 75 因素，非退休金资产按 62.5/37.5 偏向妻分配。其中一笔存于「受控资金账户」（Controlled Money Account）的款项，在庭审时被列为固定金额 566,055 澳元，法院遂按固定数额分割（妻 309,536、余额归夫）。

问题在于：法官当时不知道新南威尔士州的受控资金账户会计息，也无人提示该数额会变动。到要分钱时，账户已涨到约 575,766 澳元（比庭审时多约 9,711）。妻于 2025-12-10 申请依规则 10.13/10.14 更正终局令，使她能按比例分得含利息的份额；并请求夫承担她自 2023-10-17（她发出和解要约之日）起的费用。

法官直言「沮丧」：双方为 9,711 澳元争执，半年间律师费却共增 121,246 澳元，称两人「嗜讼（a thirst for litigation）」。

CasePocket：最终 Anderson 法官——① 用笔误更正规则 r 10.13(e)（命令不反映法院本意）撤销并以比例公式替换该分割令，使利息一并按 62.5% 归妻；② 认定调解中共同编制的资产负债表就费用问题可采（《证据法》s 131(2)(h)）；③ 依 s 114UB 判夫承担妻自 2023-10-17 起的费用，并预留 11.2 万澳元待评估。
html.en:
This is the sequel to the same property dispute. On 12 Nov 2025 Anderson J made final property orders: weighing contributions (57.5/42.5 to the wife) and the s 75 factors, the non-superannuation assets were divided 62.5/37.5 in the wife's favour. One sum sat in a Controlled Money Account and, at trial, was recorded as a fixed $566,055; the court divided it by fixed amounts ($309,536 to the wife, the balance to the husband).

The catch: the judge did not know that in New South Wales a Controlled Money Account accrues interest, and no one flagged that the figure would move. By the time the money was to be paid out, the account had grown to about $575,766 (≈ $9,711 more than at trial). On 10 Dec 2025 the wife applied under rr 10.13/10.14 to correct the final orders so she would receive her proportionate share including interest; she also sought the husband to pay her costs from 17 Oct 2023 (the date of her settlement offer).

The judge said he was "dispirited": the parties were fighting over $9,711 while their legal fees had grown by $121,246 in six months — they had "a thirst for litigation".

CasePocket: Anderson J ultimately — (1) used the slip rule, r 10.13(e) (an order that does not reflect the court's intention) to set aside and replace the division with a proportionate formula, so the interest too goes 62.5% to the wife; (2) held a balance sheet jointly prepared at mediation was admissible on costs (Evidence Act s 131(2)(h)); and (3) under s 114UB ordered the husband to pay the wife's costs from 17 Oct 2023, preserving $112,000 pending assessment.

hint.en: The judge's path, step by step

t.zh: 判决路径 · 案件走向

html.zh:
1
妻的救济途径？非 r 10.13(g) 笔误、亦非 (h) 意外疏漏；夫提出可上诉或依 s 79A 申请撤销。法官认为应考虑 r 10.13(e)——命令不反映法院本意。 ›
2
笔误规则的边界：只能用于「不存在真正分歧」之更正，不能重开裁量（Jepson No 9 / Pawley）；法官本意须清晰、错误可更正（Zan）。 ›
3
规则目的（Broda）：避免不公、确保命令反映法院宣判时的本意，即便命令已正式生效亦可更正。 ›
4
套用本案：法官本意是非退休金资产 62.5/37.5 偏向妻；固定金额的分割令遗漏了其后所生利息 → 撤销 Order 2，改以比例公式替换，并据 r 10.14 对判决理由作相应修正。
5
证据：调解中共同编制的资产负债表受 《证据法》s 131(1) 特免保护；但 s 131(2)(h) 例外——「与认定费用责任相关」者不受限（Silver Fox）；相关性依 s 55 判断（不被 s 102NL 排除）→ 就费用问题可采。 ›
6
费用 s 114UB：存在「正当情形」→ 判夫承担妻自 2023-10-17（要约之日）起的财产之诉费用（当事人对当事人，协商或评估）；妻就本申请完全胜诉亦获费用；预留 11.2 万澳元待评估。 ›
html.en:
1
The wife's route? Not r 10.13(g) clerical mistake, nor (h) accidental slip; the husband suggested an appeal or an application under s 79A. The judge considered r 10.13(e) — an order that does not reflect the court's intention. ›
2
The slip rule's limits: only for corrections on which there is no real difference of opinion, and it cannot reopen discretion (Jepson No 9 / Pawley); the judge's intention must be clear and the error amenable (Zan). ›
3
The rule's purpose (Broda): to avoid injustice and ensure the order reflects the court's intention at publication — available even after the orders are perfected. ›
4
Applied here: the intention was a 62.5/37.5 split of non-super assets favouring the wife; the fixed-dollar order omitted the interest later accruing → set aside Order 2 and replace it with a proportionate formula, with consequential r 10.14 amendments to the reasons.
5
Evidence: the balance sheet jointly prepared at mediation is protected by Evidence Act s 131(1); but s 131(2)(h) excepts material "relevant to determining liability for costs" (Silver Fox); relevance is judged under s 55 (not ousted by s 102NL) → admissible on costs. ›
6
Costs s 114UB: justifying circumstances exist → the husband pays the wife's costs of the property litigation from 17 Oct 2023 (the offer date) (party-and-party, agreed or assessed); the wife, wholly successful on the applications, also gets those costs; $112,000 preserved pending assessment. ›

hint.en: Why the judge decided this way

html.zh:
1. 为何用 r 10.13(e) 而非 (g)/(h)。法官认为这不是「笔误（clerical mistake）」或「意外疏漏（accidental slip or omission）」；但命令不反映法院本意这一项（(e)）适用。其本意见于判决理由：贡献 57.5/42.5、非退休金资产 62.5/37.5 偏向妻——而固定金额的分割未能涵盖账户其后所生利息。

2. 笔误规则的边界，本案为何能用。据 Jepson（援引 Pawley/Elyard），笔误规则只适用于不存在真正分歧、且不涉及法官裁量之更正；Zan 要求法官本意清晰、错误可更正；Broda 则确认其目的是避免不公、确保命令反映宣判本意，即便命令已正式生效仍可更正，并可由初审或上诉法院为之。本案的更正只是把已确定的比例套到含利息的账户，未重开任何裁量，故适用。法官并据 r 10.14 对判决理由相应段落作了一致性修正。

3. 调解资产负债表为何可用于费用。该表为 2023-10-17 调解中共同编制，原则上受 《证据法》s 131(1)（和解磋商特免）保护；但 s 131(2)(h) 例外——「与认定费用责任相关」之沟通/文件不受限。法官采 Silver Fox（Mansfield 法官）之说：磋商内容在仅处理费用时应可受检视，当事人不能借「without prejudice」或调解协议规避；相关性依 s 55 判断，且该判断不被家事法 s 102NL 排除。结论：该表就费用问题相关、可采。

4. 费用。法官满意存在作出费用令的正当情形（含妻 2023-10-17 的合理要约），判夫承担妻自该日起财产之诉的当事人对当事人费用（协商或评估），并就本系列申请（妻完全胜诉）另判费用；为确保受偿，预留 11.2 万澳元于夫方律师信托账户待评估。
html.en:
1. Why r 10.13(e) and not (g)/(h). The judge held this was not a "clerical mistake" or "accidental slip or omission"; but the limb that an order does not reflect the court's intention ((e)) applied. That intention appears in the reasons: contributions 57.5/42.5 and a 62.5/37.5 split of non-super assets favouring the wife — which the fixed-dollar division failed to capture once the account kept accruing interest.

2. The slip rule's limits, and why it fit. Per Jepson (citing Pawley/Elyard), the slip rule applies only to corrections on which there is no real difference of opinion and which do not involve the judge's discretion; Zan requires the intention to be clear and the error amenable; Broda confirms its purpose — to avoid injustice and ensure the order reflects the intention at publication, available even after orders are perfected, and deployable by the primary or appellate court. Here the correction merely applies the already-fixed proportion to the interest-bearing account, reopening no discretion, so it fit. Consequential r 10.14 amendments were made to the reasons.

3. Why the mediation balance sheet is usable on costs. Prepared jointly at the 17 Oct 2023 mediation, it is in principle protected by Evidence Act s 131(1) (settlement-negotiation privilege); but s 131(2)(h) excepts communications/documents "relevant to determining liability for costs". The judge adopted Silver Fox (Mansfield J): negotiation content should be open to inspection when only costs are in issue, and a party cannot avoid that by "without prejudice" labels or a mediation agreement; relevance is judged under s 55, a judgment not ousted by FLA s 102NL. The balance sheet was therefore relevant and admissible on costs.

4. Costs. The judge was satisfied there were justifying circumstances for a costs order (including the wife's reasonable offer of 17 Oct 2023) and ordered the husband to pay the wife's party-and-party costs of the property litigation from that date (agreed or assessed), plus the costs of this round of applications (on which the wife wholly succeeded); to secure payment, $112,000 was preserved in the husband's solicitor's trust account pending assessment.

hint.en: What you can cite it for

t.zh: 本案确立什么 · 可援引于
t.en: What this case establishes · cite for

html.zh:
确立/重申：

① r 10.13(e) 的笔误更正——当命令不反映法院本意时可更正，区别于 (g) 笔误与 (h) 意外疏漏；即便命令已正式生效亦可；但不得重开裁量或更正存在真正分歧之事项（Broda / Zan / Jepson / Pawley）。把已确定的比例套用到变动中的资产（如计息账户），属正当运用。

② 调解/和解磋商文件原则上受《证据法》s 131(1) 保护，但仅就费用问题可依 s 131(2)(h) 采纳；「without prejudice」标注或调解保密协议不能排除此例外；相关性依 s 55 判断，且不被家事法 s 102NL 排除（Silver Fox）。

③ 费用（s 114UB）可自一方发出合理和解要约之日起算（卡尔德班克式要约的影响）。

可援引于：用笔误规则修补「漏算利息/变动数额」的财产令；在费用之争中援用调解中的要约/资产负债表；以及主张自要约之日起的费用。
html.en:
Establishes / reaffirms:

(1) The r 10.13(e) slip rule — correction where an order does not reflect the court's intention, distinct from (g) clerical mistake and (h) accidental slip; available even after orders are perfected; but it cannot reopen discretion or correct matters of real dispute (Broda / Zan / Jepson / Pawley). Applying an already-fixed proportion to a moving asset (e.g. an interest-bearing account) is a proper use.

(2) Mediation/settlement-negotiation documents are in principle protected by Evidence Act s 131(1), but are admissible on costs only under s 131(2)(h); a "without prejudice" label or a mediation confidentiality agreement cannot defeat that exception; relevance is judged under s 55 and is not ousted by FLA s 102NL (Silver Fox).

(3) Costs (s 114UB) may run from the date a party made a reasonable settlement offer (the Calderbank-type effect).

Cite it for: using the slip rule to fix property orders that miss interest/moving figures; deploying a mediation offer/balance sheet in a costs fight; and seeking costs from the date of an offer.

t.zh: FCFCOA（家事法）规则 2021 — r 10.13（笔误更正规则）

sc.zh: 本案：更正终局令的依据
sc.en: In case: basis for correcting the orders

desc.zh: 法院可随时变更或撤销命令，若该命令：(a) 在一方缺席时作出；(b) 以欺诈取得；(c) 属临时性；(d) 系禁制令或委任接管人；(e) 不反映法院本意；(f) 受益一方同意；(g) 存在笔误（clerical mistake）；或 (h) 因意外疏漏（accidental slip or omission）而生错误。本案依 (e) 更正。
desc.en: The court may at any time vary or set aside an order if it: (a) was made in a party's absence; (b) was obtained by fraud; (c) is interlocutory; (d) is an injunction/receiver order; (e) does not reflect the intention of the court; (f) the party in whose favour it was made consents; (g) contains a clerical mistake; or (h) is affected by an accidental slip or omission. The court relied on (e).

src.zh: 法院认定非 (g)/(h)，而依 (e)「不反映法院本意」撤销并替换该分割令。
src.en: The court held it was not (g)/(h) but relied on (e) — 'does not reflect the intention of the court' — to set aside and replace the order.

sc.zh: 本案：撤销财产令的替代途径
sc.en: In case: alternative route to set aside

desc.zh: 法院可在特定情形下（如出现司法不公、欺诈、未披露、情势重大变化、或令难以执行等）撤销或变更已作出的 s 79 财产令。夫曾提出妻可循 s 79A，但法院最终采笔误更正规则。
desc.en: The court may, in defined circumstances (e.g. a miscarriage of justice, fraud, non-disclosure, a significant change of circumstances, or impracticability), set aside or vary a s 79 property order. The husband suggested s 79A; the court instead used the slip rule.

src.zh: 夫主张妻应循上诉或 s 79A；法院认为 r 10.13(e) 更为适切。
src.en: The husband argued for an appeal or s 79A; the court found r 10.13(e) the apt course.

sc.zh: 本案：和解磋商特免及其费用例外
sc.en: In case: settlement privilege & costs exception

desc.zh: 原则上，为达成纠纷和解而进行的沟通或编制的文件不得举证（s 131(1)）；但设有例外，其中 s 131(2)(h)：若沟通/文件「与认定费用责任相关」，则不受该限制。本案据此采纳调解资产负债表用于费用。
desc.en: As a rule, communications made or documents prepared in an attempt to settle a dispute must not be adduced (s 131(1)); but there are exceptions, including s 131(2)(h): the prohibition does not apply if the communication/document is 'relevant to determining liability for costs'. The balance sheet was admitted on costs under this gateway.

src.zh: 法院据 s 131(2)(h) 与 Silver Fox 采纳调解资产负债表用于费用。
src.en: The court admitted the mediation balance sheet on costs via s 131(2)(h) and Silver Fox.

desc.zh: 证据若「可合理影响」对争议中事实存在与否的评估，即具相关性。资产负债表是否「与费用相关」即依 s 55 判断；该判断不被家事法 s 102NL 排除。
desc.en: Evidence is relevant if it could rationally affect the assessment of the probability of a fact in issue. Whether the balance sheet is 'relevant to costs' is judged under s 55; that judgment is not ousted by FLA s 102NL.

src.zh: 法院据 s 55 认定该资产负债表与费用相关。
src.en: Under s 55 the court found the balance sheet relevant to costs.

desc.zh: 在适用本分编的某些程序中，除非法院另有决定，否则不适用证据规则。法院指出：s 102NL 并不排除依《证据法》s 55 判断相关性。
desc.en: In certain proceedings to which the Division applies, the rules of evidence do not apply unless the court decides otherwise. The court noted s 102NL does not oust the s 55 relevance inquiry.

src.zh: 法院澄清 s 102NL 不排除 s 55 的相关性判断。
src.en: The court clarified s 102NL does not displace the s 55 relevance test.

desc.zh: 一般规则为各自负担费用；但在「公正」之情形，法院可作其认为公正的费用命令，并须顾及 (3) 列因素（双方财务、行为、是否完全败诉、书面和解要约等）——任一因素即可能具决定性。
desc.en: The general rule is each party bears their own costs; but where 'just', the court may make any costs order it considers just, having regard to the (3) factors (finances, conduct, whether wholly unsuccessful, written settlement offers, etc.) — any one of which may be determinative.

src.zh: 法院满意存在正当情形，据 s 114UB 判夫承担妻自 2023-10-17 起的费用。
src.en: Satisfied justifying circumstances existed, the court ordered the husband to pay the wife's costs from 17 Oct 2023 under s 114UB.

summary.zh: Austin 法官阐述 r 10.13：可依 (e)/(g)/(h) 更正命令以确保当事人获其应得；笔误规则旨在避免不公、确保判决反映法院宣判时本意，即便命令已正式生效亦可更正，并可由初审或上诉法院为之（援引 Gould v Vaggelas、NH v DPP、Achurch）。
summary.en: Austin J explained r 10.13: an order may be corrected under (e)/(g)/(h) to ensure a party receives their proper entitlement; the slip rule avoids injustice and ensures the judgment reflects the court's intention at publication, available even after orders are perfected, and deployable by the primary or appellate court (citing Gould v Vaggelas; NH v DPP; Achurch).

prop.zh: 笔误规则的目的与可及范围（含已生效命令）。
prop.en: Purpose and reach of the slip rule (incl. perfected orders).

Broda.passage: "The availability of the slip rule is to avoid injustice by ensuring the judgment reflects the Court's intention when judgment was published and to prevent unintended consequences… The correction of an order, even after it is finally perfected, is permissible to ensure the Court record represents what the Court intended to pronounce."

src.zh: 本案据 Broda 确立可用笔误规则更正已生效命令。
src.en: The case relied on Broda for correcting perfected orders by the slip rule.

note.zh: ✦ 上诉判例（未纳入本资料库）。

court.zh: FCFCOA 第 1 庭（全席上诉）· 2023

summary.zh: 全席法院指出：唯有当初审法官的本意清晰、且错误可经笔误规则更正时，r 10.13 方可适用。
summary.en: The Full Court held r 10.13 applies only where the trial judge's intention is clear and the error is amenable to correction by the slip rule.

prop.zh: 适用笔误规则的前提：本意清晰、错误可更正。
prop.en: Preconditions: clear intention and an amenable error.

summary.zh: 副首席法官 McClelland 援引 Pawley/Elyard：笔误规则只能用于「不存在真正分歧」之更正，不能涉及法官裁量；当某争点在终局庭审中存在分歧、对方未获质证机会时，不宜以 r 10.13(e) 变更。
summary.en: Deputy Chief Justice McClelland (citing Pawley/Elyard): the slip rule applies only where no real difference of opinion exists and cannot involve the judge's discretion; where an issue was contested at the final hearing and the other party had no chance to test the evidence, r 10.13(e) should not be used.

prop.zh: 笔误规则不得重开裁量或有真正分歧之事项。
prop.en: The slip rule cannot reopen discretion or genuinely disputed matters.

src.zh: 本案据 Jepson 区分：此处仅套用已定比例、未重开裁量，故可用。
src.en: Per Jepson, here only a fixed proportion was applied (no discretion reopened), so it could be used.

summary.zh: Mansfield 法官：和解/调解磋商之要约，原则上因 s 131(1) 不得举证；但 s 131(2)(h) 使其在仅处理费用时可受检视；当事人不能以「without prejudice」或保密条款规避此项暴露。
summary.en: Mansfield J: settlement/mediation offers are prima facie inadmissible under s 131(1); but s 131(2)(h) exposes them to inspection when only costs are in issue; a party cannot avoid that exposure by 'without prejudice' labels or confidentiality terms.

prop.zh: s 131(2)(h)：磋商内容就费用问题可采。
prop.en: s 131(2)(h): negotiation content is admissible on costs.

SilverFox.passage: "There is no apparent public interest in permitting a party to avoid such exposure by imposing terms upon the communication, whether by the use of the expression 'without prejudice' or by a mediation agreement."

summary.en: Established the 'Calderbank offer': a party may make a written settlement offer (reserving the right to rely on it as to costs); if rejected and the result is no better than the offer, it can affect the costs outcome.

summary.zh: Spigelman 首席大法官：保留的费用命令常因疏忽未及处理、久之被遗忘；其后处理并非不当。本案据此否定夫「妻不当翻起保留费用」之异议。
summary.en: Spigelman CJ: reserved costs orders are often, by oversight, not dealt with and forgotten with time; later dealing with them is not improper. The court used this to reject the husband's 'impermissibly agitating reserved costs' objection.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ANDERSON J:

INTRODUCTION

The parties’ competing applications for financial orders pursuant to Part VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) were finalised on 12 November 2025 when I made final financial orders and delivered Reasons for Judgment.[1] My reasons for judgment provide context to the dispute between the parties.
On 10 December 2025, the Applicant Wife (“the wife”) filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking orders for amendments to be made to the final orders and Reasons for Judgment pursuant to Rules 10.13 and 10.14 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (“the Rules”). The Respondent Husband (“the husband”) agreed to the making of some amendments to each of the final orders and Reasons for Judgment and those amendments were made on 10 February 2026. However, the parties do not agree whether the provisions of r 10.13 of the Rules known in legal parlance as the “slip rule” can be deployed to amend the final orders to ensure that interest on monies held in a Controlled Money Account subsequent to the tender of an Amended Balance Sheet on 24 September 2025[2] be divided between the parties.
In addition, and on 10 December 2025, the wife by her Application in a Proceeding sought the costs of and incidental to two interlocutory applications filed by the husband on 16 June 2025 and 19 September 2025. On 23 January 2026, the wife amended her application and instead of the costs of the interlocutory applications, the wife sought the costs of and incidental to services provided by her legal representatives from the time that the wife made an offer in an effort to resolve the competing financial applications on 17 October 2023.
These reasons explain why I have determined to:
(a) Employ the terms of r 10.13(e) of the Rules to make amendments to my final orders and Reasons for Judgment to ensure that the terms of the orders are just and equitable; and

(b) Make an order for the wife’s costs fixed on a party and party basis such costs to be agreed or assessed.

ISSUES TO BE DETERMINED

Having reviewed the terms of the parties’ competing applications, the affidavits filed by each of them as well as written submissions prepared and filed by the wife’s solicitor advocate and the husband’s counsel, the issues, which I am called upon to determine include the following:
(a) Whether the Court ought to deploy the slip rule to ensure that interest on moneys held in a Controlled Money Account be apportioned between the parties in a manner, which the wife says is consistent with my Reasons for Judgment;

(b) Whether the wife’s application filed on 10 December 2025 impermissibly seeks to agitate reserved cost orders, which were not the subject of argument at the time of the final hearing and not addressed by the final orders. Further, if such an application was impermissible, whether the application was a “nullity” as so described by the husband’s counsel;[3]

(c) Whether the wife is precluded from amending the application filed on 10 December 2025 in circumstances where the husband says that the application was a “nullity” in any event;

(d) Whether the wife is precluded from seeking an order for the payment of her costs of and incidental to work performed by her legal representatives subsequent to 17 October 2023 in circumstances where such an application was not made until 23 January 2026 being in excess of the time permitted by r 12.13(3)(b) of the Rules;

(e) If the answer to the preceding question is in the negative, whether the wife is permitted to rely on in support of her application for costs, a Balance Sheet, which was prepared by the parties during a private mediation held on the same day as the offer, which the wife conveyed by letter dated 17 October 2023;

(f) If the answer to the preceding question is in the affirmative, whether I ought to exercise my discretion to make an order for costs;[4] and

(g) If the answer to the preceding question is in the affirmative, whether the Court should award the wife a specific amount as sought by her or otherwise, make an order requiring the wife’s costs to be assessed in accordance with Schedule 3 to the Rules.

EVIDENCE

The wife relied on:
(a) An Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 23 January 2026;

(b) Affidavits in the name of the wife filed on 23 January 2026 and 10 March 2026;

(c) An Outline of Case Document filed on 9 February 2026;

(d) An Outline of Case Document filed on 22 March 2026; and

(e) Some tendered documents.[5]

The husband relied on:
(a) An Amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 5 February 2026;

(b) Affidavit in the name of the husband filed on 9 January 2026 and 24 February 2026; and

(c) An Outline of Case Document filed on 20 March 2026.

Although the wife tendered some documents at the hearing on 10 February 2026, the parties by their counsel at the hearing on 24 March 2026 agreed to dispense with the terms of r 8.15(3)(e) of the Rules.

FIRST ISSUE – VARIATION OF THE FINAL ORDERS

On 12 November 2025, I made an order in the following terms:
In respect of the sum of $566,055 held in a Controlled Money Account, the proceeds be applied in the following manner:
(a) A sum of $309,536 to the Trust Account of the solicitor for the Respondent Wife (“the wife”) for and on behalf of the wife;

(b) The balance then remaining to the Trust Account of the solicitor for the Applicant Husband (“the husband”) for and on behalf of the husband.

I made the order in circumstances where:
(a) At the time the parties tendered an Amended Balance Sheet during Closing Submissions,[6] the monies held in the “Unified Lawyers ITF Controlled Money Account [#92]” were described in a fix sum, namely, a sum of $566,055;

(b) I was not aware that in the State of New South Wales, a Controlled Money Account accrues interest; and

(c) I did not receive any documentary evidence or oral submission about the nature of the Controlled Money Account or any warning that the sum referred to in the Amended Balance Sheet may alter prior to the delivery of final judgment.

Subsequent to the making of final orders on 12 November 2025, I learned by way of an affidavit in the name of the wife filed on 23 January 2026 that between the time of Closing Submissions on 24 September 2025 and 1 January 2026, the monies in the Controlled Money Account had increased from $566,055 to $572,516.99.[7] At the time of the hearing on 24 March 2026, the wife’s counsel advised me that the sum had increased to $575,766 being a sum equating to $9,711 more than the quantum attributed to the Controlled Money Account at the time of Closing Submissions on 24 September 2025. This being so, and in circumstances where I made an order dividing the monies in the Controlled Money Account by reference to fixed amounts, it was the effect of the wife’s submission that:
(a) The orders caused the wife an injustice; and

(b) The orders did not reflect my intention at the time that I made my Orders on 12 November 2025.

The argument put by the wife and the husband’s counterargument are made in circumstances where:
(a) Since 22 September 2025, the wife’s legal fees have increased from $131,763 to $232,471;[8] and

(b) Since 21 September 2025, the husband’s legal fees have increased from $229,338 to $249,876.

I make this observation to highlight my dissatisfaction that the parties as at 24 March 2026 were arguing about a sum of $9,711 in circumstances where in the space of six months, the parties’ legal fees have increased collectively by a sum of $121,246. At the hearing on 24 March 2026, I remarked that the parties have “a thirst for litigation”. It is dispiriting that the parties’ efforts were not focused on resolving the differences between them in circumstances where firstly, the parties separated almost five years ago[9] and secondly, the litigation commenced over three years ago.[10]
In any event, I have reviewed the Reasons for Judgment, which I delivered on 12 November 2025. It is clear from my Reasons that:
(a) I considered that a fixed sum of $566,055 was available for division between the parties;[11]

(b) I assessed the parties’ contributions and current and future circumstances by reference to the parties’ existing assets as set out at [68] of the Reasons. Indeed, and at [103] of my Reasons, I say as follows with respect to the parties’ contributions:

Having regard to the significant contributions made by the wife’s family during the relationship, the receipt of a significant inheritance in the context of a modest balance sheet late in the relationship and the wife’s post-separation contributions, I assess overall contributions as favouring her in the order of 57.5/42.5 percent.[12]

(emphasis added)
(c) At [109] and [117] of my Reasons, I carefully considered the form of the orders to be made by me and the effect of my orders vis-à-vis each party. The analysis is performed having regard to the fixed Balance Sheet, which I adopted at [68] and [69] of my Reasons.
The wife by her Amended Application in a Proceeding and her solicitor advocate’s Outline of Case Document submits that I ought to vary the final orders made by me such that:
(a) The wife receive 54.68 percent of the monies held in the Controlled Money Account; and

(b) The husband receive 45.32 percent of the monies held in the Controlled Money Account.

These percentages are calculated by reference to the following contention:
The wife’s share of $309,536 equates to 54.68% of the controlled monies account, with the husband’s share of the balance of $256,519 (as it then was as at 18 September 2025, four days prior to the commencement of the trial on the 22nd [of September 2025] equating to 45.32%.

The figures in the immediately preceding point – as part of the non-super pool of assets – were meant to effect at 62.5 : 37.5 adjustment in favour of the wife with which she does not cavil.[13]
For his part, the husband’s counsel submitted at the hearing on 24 March 2026 that the appropriate remedy available to the wife would have been to:
(a) Appeal from the Orders, which I made on 12 November 2025; or

(b) File an application to vary or set aside the Orders made on 12 November 2025 pursuant to s 79A of the Act. No submission was made as to the merit or otherwise of such an application.

In support of the wife’s application, the wife’s solicitor advocate relied on sub-paragraphs (g) and (h) of rule 10.13 of the Rules. Rule 10.13 of the Rules reads as follows:
(1) The court may at any time vary or set aside an order, if:
(a) it was made in the absence of a party; or

(b) it was obtained by fraud; or

(c) it is interlocutory; or

(d) it is an injunction or for the appointment of a receiver; or

(e) it does not reflect the intention of the court; or

(f) the party in whose favour it was made consents; or

(g) there is a clerical mistake in the order; or

(h) there is an error arising in the order from an accidental slip or omission.
(2) Subrule (1) does not affect the power of the court to vary or terminate the operation of an order by a further order.
I do not consider that r 10.13(g) or r 10.13(h) of the Rules provide the wife with the remedy sought by her. Particularly, I do not consider that I made a “clerical mistake”, nor do I consider that I made “an accidental slip or omission”.
However, the wife’s application itself refers to r 10.13 of the Rules generally. This being so, I consider that r 10.13(e) warrants consideration.
The rule fell for consideration by the Honourable Deputy Chief Justice McClelland in Jepson & Jepson (No 9) [2025] FedCFamC1F 659. I brought counsel’s attention to that decision at the hearing on 10 February 2026. The reasons delivered by the Deputy Chief Justice identify that in that case, his Honour was concerned with the wife’s application for the variation of final property orders such that the amount payable by the wife to the husband be reduced.[14] Particularly, the wife’s counsel argued that a sum of $147,415 in legal fees paid to the wife’s counsel by the wife “were incorrectly added back to Pool 1, despite being paid from Pool 2”. The wife’s counsel further contended that “if the add back was correctly allocated to Pool 2, the amount payable to the husband by the wife would be reduced by half of those fees, being a reduction of $73,707”.[15]
At paragraphs 15 to 17 of his judgment, the Deputy Chief Justice said as follows:
[15] In Pawley & Pawley (No 2) (2017) FLC 93-787, Thackray J explained that the slip rule can only be applied where no real difference of opinion exists about the proposed amendment. At [55] his Honour stated:
Furthermore, the authorities make plain that the slip rule can be applied only where the amendment is one upon which no real difference of opinion can exist. Hence, it cannot apply where there is any question involving the exercise of discretion by the judge: Elyard Corporation Pty Ltd v DDB Needham Sydney Pty Ltd [1995] FCA 943; (1995) 61 FCR 385 at 390-391. This proposition is true not only at common law but applies also to matters in which rules equivalent to r 16.05 apply (for example, r 39.05 of the Federal Court Rules 2011 (Cth) and its predecessor, Order 35 r 7 of the Federal Court Rules 1979 (Cth)).
[16] In the present case, there was a difference of opinion between the parties regarding the treatment of add backs at the Final Hearing. There remains such a difference of opinion. It would be unfair to the husband to make the variation sought by the wife in circumstances where the issue was not addressed at Final Hearing and the husband was not availed with the opportunity of testing any evidence presented by the wife regarding the source of the funds nor the significance of payment from that source.

[17] Accordingly, this is not a matter where it is appropriate to exercise power pursuant to r 10.13(e) of the Rules.

It is clear from the reasons delivered by the Deputy Chief Justice that in circumstances where there was a difference of opinion between the parties regarding the treatment of “addbacks” at the final hearing, it would be unfair to the husband to make the variation sought by the wife. When arriving at that conclusion, the Deputy Chief Justice identified that:
(a) The treatment of the “addbacks” remained a contentious issue at the time of hearing the wife’s application for a variation of his final orders; and

(b) The husband “was not availed with the opportunity of testing” the wife’s contention about the source of funds for the payment of the wife’s counsel fees.

The application of r 10.13 of the Rules also fell for consideration by Justices Austin, Tree and Christie in Zan & Wen (No 2) [2023] FedCFamC1A 130 (“Zan”). The reasons delivered by their Honours identify that the rule can only be applied if the intentions of the trial judge are clear and the error(s) is amenable to correct pursuant to the slip rule.[16]
Subsequent to Zan and in Broda & Broda [2025] FedCFamC1A 218 (“Broda”), Justice Austin said as follows with respect to the terms of r 10.13 of the Rules:
[20] The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) provide that an order of the Court may be varied at any time if it does not reflect the intention of the Court (r 10.13(1)(e)), there is a clerical mistake in the order (r 10.13(1)(g)), or the order is beset by an error caused by an accidental slip (r 10.13(1)(h)). Each of the sub-rules could be deployed to rectify Order 4(c) to ensure the wife receives her proper entitlement if the husband’s real property must be sold due to his default and it realises a sale price of less than $110,000.

[21] The availability of the slip rule is to avoid injustice by ensuring the judgment reflects the Court’s intention when judgment was published and to prevent unintended consequences (Gould v Vaggelas (1985) 157 CLR 215 at 275). The correction of an order, even after it is finally perfected, is permissible to ensure the Court record represents what the Court intended to pronounce (NH v Director of Public Prosecutions (SA) [2016] HCA 33; (2016) 260 CLR 546 at [67]–[71]; Achurch v The Queen [2014] HCA 10; (2014) 253 CLR 141 at [14]–[27]). Those principles certainly have current application.

[22] On past occasions, the Full Court has observed how perfected orders may by varied by use of the slip rule to correct errors without the appeal needing to be necessarily allowed (Agius & Agius [2010] FamCAFC 143; (2010) FLC 93-442 at [169]–[175]; King v Kemp [1996] FamCA 24; (1996) FLC 92-673 at 83,010). The slip rule may be deployed by either the appellate court (Gilles & Irby [2016] FamCAFC 13; (2016) FLC 93-687 at [10]–[20]; Goudarzi & Bagheri (No 2) [2017] FamCAFC 190 at [57]–[62]) or the primary judge.

In Broda, the primary judge refused to amend an order pursuant to r 10.13 in circumstances where the operative orders made no provision for the wife’s payment of her share if the husband’s real property sold for less than $110,000. That is, the primary judge’s order only made provision for the wife if the real property sold for exactly $110,000 or for more than $110,000.[17] Justice Austin identified the fundamental reason why the primary judge fell into legal error by summarily dismissing the wife’s application, namely, that the orders did not properly reflect the decision made by the primary judge that the wife should receive 67.5 per cent of the net value of the parties’ assets and superannuation.[18] Particularly, and at [24] of his Judgment, Justice Austin said as follows:
[24] True enough, the primary judge’s error was induced by the infelicitous drafting of proposed orders by the wife’s lawyers, but that error did not excuse the primary judge from the legal obligation making just and equitable orders to finalise the cause of action. The orders evidently were not just and equitable because they do not ensure the wife receives her 67.5 per cent share of the property in the event of the husband’s default (which has already occurred) and the potentiality of his real property not realising a gross sale of price of at least $110,000.[19]
(emphasis)
More recently, and in Hunt Leather Pty Ltd & anor v Transport for NSW [No 2] [2026] HCA 4, the High Court of Australia said as follows with respect to its power to recall and amend orders:
[5] It is unnecessary on this application to address the precise source of power by which applications to recall and amend orders of this Court are made. One source is r 3.01.2 of the High Court Rules 2004 (Cth), which provides that "[t]he Court or a Justice may, at any time, correct a clerical mistake in a judgment or order, or an error arising in a judgment or order from any accidental slip or omission". A broader source may be the powers of this Court which are incidental and necessary to the exercise of its jurisdiction. Whatever the source of power, the authorities have recognised that this Court has, at least, a discretionary power to correct an error or omission in a judgment or order where "the interests of justice so require". Nevertheless, the discretionary power to correct an error or omission is to be exercised "sparingly" due to the interests of finality, with a "heavy burden ... cast upon the applicant".
(citations omitted)
In the same way as the High Court, this Court has an inherent power to make orders, which are necessary to control its own processes and to prevent injustice to a litigant.[20]
The High Court went on to identify what it described as the “strongest factors that support the exercise of the discretionary power to correct an error or omission”. These factors included:
(a) Where the error or omission is plain or obvious; and

(b) Where the application is not brought for the purpose of “re-agitating arguments already considered by the Court”.

Having regard to the decision of the High Court but more particularly, the decision of Justice Austin in Broda, I am persuaded that it is appropriate in this case to amend my orders such that the wife receive 62.5 percent of the non-superannuation assets available for distribution including the interest, which has accrued in the Controlled Money Account between the time of Closing Submissions on 24 September 2025 and the current time. This is because my error is obvious having regard to the terms of [68], [69], [103] and [109] to [117] of my Reasons for Judgment. Particularly, it is plain that I was motivated to ensure that the wife receive 62.5 percent of the non-superannuation assets available for distribution.
I am concerned that the amendments proposed by the wife’s Amended Application in a Proceeding will only serve to cause further confusion by those persons charged with implementing my orders. This being so, and pursuant to rule 10.13(e) of the Rules I will set aside Order 2 of the Orders made by me on 12 November 2025 and replace it with a formula, which recognises that the wife is to receive 62.5 percent of the non-superannuation assets available for distribution (including the interest, which has accrued in the Controlled Money Account) but also taking into account the assets in the wife’s name at the time of delivery of my final judgment. Pursuant to r 10.14 of the Rules, I will make consequential amendments to paragraphs 112 and 113 of my Reasons for Judgment to reflect that the wife is to receive 62.5 percent of the non-superannuation assets such assets to include the quantum of the Controlled Money Account as it may stand at the time that the monies are disbursed. A similar amendment will be required to paragraph 113 of my Reasons for Judgment to reflect that the husband is to receive 37.5 percent of the non-superannuation assets such assets to include the monies in the Controlled Money Account as it stands at the time of the disbursal of the monies. Given that paragraphs 112 and 113 of my Reasons for Judgment by necessity refer to paragraphs 68 and 116, I will also make consequential amendments to those paragraphs.

SECOND ISSUE – DID THE WIFE IMPERSSIBLY SEEK TO AGITATE RESERVED COST ORDERS?

On 10 December 2025, the wife filed an Application in a Proceeding at which time she sought an order in the following terms:
That from the Husband’s share of final property settlement cash proceeds, the sum of $8,195.67 be deducted and paid to the Wife by way of costs in relation to the Husband’s Applications in a Proceeding, filed 16 June 2025 and 19 September 2025.
The wife’s application was filed in circumstances where on 19 June 2025, I reserved the costs of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed on 16 June 2025 as well as the costs of the hearing that day. Similarly, and on 22 September 2025, I reserved the costs of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed on 19 September 2025 together with the costs of the hearing thrown away that day.
The husband invites me to dismiss the wife’s application on the basis that the wife “impermissibly seeks to agitate reserved costs orders that were not dealt with at the trial”.[21] In support of that contention, the husband referred to a decision delivered by the former Chief Justice of New South Wales, namely, Chief Justice Speigelman, in New South Wales Bar Association v Clarence James Stevens [2005] NSWCA 351 (“New South Wales Bar Association”). In that decision, the Chief Justice said as follows:
[11] The Claimant’s submissions in this Court on the Notice of Motion are candid. They state:
“It was by oversight that the reserved costs orders were not dealt with. With the lapse of time, they had been forgotten”.
[12] There are two quite distinct issues of construction.
[13] In my opinion, as a matter of construction the consent order which was ordered and entered on 19 September 2003 in proceedings No 13263 of 2002 does not encompass the costs of the proceedings before Greg James J.

[14] The position where the costs of interlocutory proceedings have been expressly reserved and were then not dealt with in the order finally disposing of the proceedings was considered in Gardner v Marshall [1845] EngR 751; (1845) 14 Sim 575. The Court held that reserved costs of interlocutory proceedings must be dealt with by a special order and were not included in the costs of the suit. This was confirmed in England 1897 in British Natural Premium Provident Assn v Bywater [1897] 2 Ch 531, 532 where Byrne J said:

The Judges of the Chancery Division have, for the guidance of the registrars and taxing masters, given the following directions in reference to the costs of interlocutory applications... Where interlocutory applications have been disposed of, but the costs have been reserved, such costs are not to be mentioned in the judgment or order, or allowed on taxation, without the special direction of the Judge.
[15] In McLaughlin v The City Bank of Sydney [1916] NSWStRp 70; (1916) 16 SR (NSW) 491, 494 Street J said:
I think the true rule in such cases is that the costs of an interlocutory application, which have been reserved, cannot be recovered by either party under an order for the taxation and payment of his costs of suit without a special direction of the Judge.
Having regard to the decision of Chief Justice Spiegelman, the husband makes four arguments:
(a) The reserved costs applications ought to have been made at the final hearing. The wife did not make such an application;

(b) An application to re-open the proceedings to have further evidence received subsequent to the delivery of the final property judgment ought to have been made by the wife;

(c) The application as filed by the wife on 10 December 2025 with respect to costs is a nullity and, on this basis, the application including the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 23 January 2026 ought to be dismissed; and

(d) In circumstances where the final property judgment has been delivered, the Court is functus officio.

The decision of Chief Justice Spiegelman was cited with approval by Justice Einstein in Idoport Pty Ltd v National Australia Bank Ltd [2006] NSWS C 895 (“Idoport”) wherein his Honour accepted that the decision is authority for the proposition that:
...an order reserving the costs of an interlocutory order is an “otherwise order” for the purpose of Pt 52A, r 16, and that therefore the award of costs in an interlocutory hearing where costs have been reserved is in the discretion of the Court.
On its face, the decisions of the New South Wales Bar Association and Idoport hold attraction. However, the decisions relate to Part 52A rule 16 of the New South Wales Supreme Court Rules, which was in the following terms:
Subject to this Part the costs of any application or other step in proceedings shall, unless the Court otherwise orders, be paid and otherwise dealt with in accordance with the provisions of this Part or the provisions of any order relating to the general costs of the proceedings.
Justice Einstein in Idoport accepted submissions made by the defendants that the question of costs of the interlocutory applications could be addressed in two ways. Either the Court could embark upon a separate determination of the respective merits of the interlocutory applications in respect of which costs had been reserved or consider those applications within the context an application for costs of the proceeding.[22] The effect of his decision is that pursuant to the New South Wales Supreme Court Rules, the discretion about how to proceed rested with the presiding judicial officer. Such a conclusion is consistent with the decision of Chief Justice Spiegelman (supra) wherein his Honour concluded that it was in the discretion of the Court whether the New South Wales Bar Association was entitled to recover its assessed costs of the interlocutory proceedings given that the proceedings had been disposed of in the Common Law Division.
For these reasons, I do not agree that the decisions of the New South Wales Bar Association or Idoport are authorities for the proposition that the wife is barred from pursuing her costs of the applications filed on 16 June 2025 and 19 September 2025. Even if such a conclusion is erroneous, I am concerned that I would fall into error by failing to consider the terms of the particular rule, which applies in this Court, namely, r 12.13 of the Rules. Rule 12.13 is in the following terms:
12.13 Order for costs
(1) The court may make an order for costs on its own initiative.

(2) A party may apply for an order that another person pay costs.

(3) An application for costs may be made:
(a) at any stage during a proceeding; or
(b) by filing an Application in a Proceeding within 28 days after the final order is made.
Note: See subsection 114UB(4) of the Family Law Act.
The wife’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 10 December 2025 was filed within the time limit prescribed by r 12.13(3)(b) of the Rules. This being so, I consider the wife’s application to be competent.

THIRD ISSUE – IS THE WIFE PRECLUDED FROM SEEKING THE PAYMENT OF HER COSTS FOR A FIXED PERIOD ON ACCOUNT OF FILING HER APPLICATION OUTSIDE OF THE TIME PERMITTED BY THE RULES?

On 23 January 2026, the wife sought to file an Amended Application in a Proceeding. By that document, the wife abandoned her application for costs in respect of the applications filed on 16 June 2025 and 19 September 2025. Instead, the wife sought an order in the following terms:
That, from the Husband’s share of final property settlement cash proceeds, the sum of $8,195.67 $109,018 be deducted and paid to the Wife by way of costs in relation to the work incurred since the Wife’s substantive offer of settlement of 17 October 2023 to the Final Orders of 12 November 2025, related only to property proceedings. Husband’s Applications in a Proceeding, filed 16 June 2025 and 19 September 2025.
That, from the Husband’s share of final property settlement cash proceedings, a sum as determined by the Honourable Court be deducted and paid to the Wife on account of the Wife’s costs of and incidental to this Amended Application in a Proceeding and the preceding application, filed 10 December 2025.
The husband submits the wife’s amended application is incompetent because it was filed seven weeks out of time. Alternatively, the husband submits that the wife requires the leave of the Court to proceed with her amended application.
I do not agree with the husband’s argument because r 2.50 of the Rules expressly permitted the wife to amend her application. Rule 2.50 is in the following terms:
2.50 Amendment by a party or court order
(1) A party who has filed an application or response may amend the application or response:
(a) for an Initiating Application (Family Law):
(i) at any time before the procedural hearing at which the proceeding is allocated a date or dates for trial; or
(ii) at a later time, with the consent of the other parties or by order;
(b) for an Application in a Proceeding:
(i) at or before the first court date; or
(ii) at any later time, with the consent of the other parties or by order; and
(c) for all other applications - at any time, with the consent of the other parties or by order.
The Application in a Proceeding filed on 10 December 2025 was allocated a hearing for the first time on 10 February 2026. The Amended Application in a Proceeding was filed on 23 January 2026. It follows that by the time of the hearing on 10 February 2026, the husband had been seized of the application for over two weeks.
The Amended Application in a Proceeding set out with particularity the orders sought by the wife. This being so, the husband cannot argue that he was prejudiced in any way at the hearing on 10 February 2026 or indeed, the hearing on 24 March 2026. As Justice Curran identified in Giacobetti & Giacobetti (No 3) [2023] FedCFamC1F 1126:
The clear and obvious intention of the Rules and orders by way of case management is to ensure that, consistent with principles of natural justice, parties have appropriate notice of the case they are required to meet.
I consider that the husband has had appropriate notice of the case he is required to meet. Further, and given the wife’s compliance with r 2.50(1)(b)(i) of the Rules, I do not consider that the wife requires leave to proceed with her Amended Application in a Proceeding.

FOURTH ISSUE: IS THE WIFE PERMITTED TO RELY ON A BALANCE SHEET PREPARED BY THE PARTIES DURING A MEDIATION IN SUPPORT OF AN APPLICATION FOR COSTS?

As identified, the wife seeks her costs in relation to work performed by her legal representatives “since the wife’s substantive offer of settlement of 17 October 2023 to the final orders of 12 November 2025, related only to property proceedings”. Parenting proceedings in relation to the two children of the relationship who were still the age of minority resolved by way of orders consented to by the parties on 16 November 2023 and 19 June 2025.
On 17 October 2023, the parties attended at a private mediation.[23]
The wife prosecutes her application for costs by reference to:
(a) A “joint balance sheet”, which the parties used at the mediation; and

(b) An offer of settlement made the same day.[24]

The wife’s letter of offer dated 17 October 2023 is expressed in the following terms:
We refer to the above matter and the mediation facilitated by [name of mediator] on 17 October 2023.

We confirm that we are instructed to propose the following offer in an attempt to resolve the matter without further litigation:
That $500,000 of the net sale proceeds of the property located at [O Street], [Suburb J] be disbursed to our client and the remainder to your client.
That our client retains the [Country B] properties, her bank accounts, [Motor Vehicle 1] , her shares, superannuation, and liabilities.
That your client retain his bank accounts, [Motor Vehicle 3], boat and trailer, [N Platform] account, superannuation, and liabilities.

We advise that this offer shall remain open for a period of fourteen days from the date of this correspondence and thereafter will lapse.

We advise that our client reserves the right to tender a copy of this letter to the Court in support of an application for costs under section 117 of the Family Law Act 1975 and will reply [sic] upon the principles of Calderbank v Calderbank (1975) 3OER222 [sic].[25]
The defining feature of a Calderbank offer is its form. Whilst an offer not in the precise form first suggested by Cairns LJ in Calderbank will not render it inadmissible or necessarily ineffective on an argument as to costs[26], a relaxation in the rigours of formality does not invite wholesale departure from the Calderbank form. A Calderbank offer should at least:
(a) Be marked without prejudice save as to costs;

(b) Be clear, precise and certain in its terms;

(c) State clearly the time in which the offer must be accepted;

(d) Make reference to the offer being one in accordance with the principles enunciated in the decision of Calderbank v Calderbank;

(e) Make some provision for costs separate from the principal offer;

(f) State clearly that the offeror reserves his/her right to tender the offer on an application for costs if the offer is rejected;

(g) State the costs advantage i.e. indemnity costs or party/party costs that the offeror has in mind to achieve; and

(h) Provide reasons why the offer should be accepted.

Although the wife’s solicitor described the offer dated 17 October 2023 as a being an offer in accordance with the principles in Calderbank, the terms of the letter fail to address the matters referred to at sub-paragraphs (e), (g) and (h) above. Nevertheless, the letter remains admissible and for the reasons discussed below, I do not consider it to be ineffective.
Further, I consider that the offer dated 17 October 2023 can be construed as an offer pursuant to the terms of r 4.06 of the Rules. That rule does not require a litigant to address the rigorous criteria identified by Calderbank.
The husband objects to the wife’s reliance on a joint balance sheet prepared during the mediation on 17 October 2023.[27] It is this document, which informed the wife’s offer of the same day. Particularly, the husband takes objection to the joint balance sheet on the basis that it is a document which is privileged by virtue of the provisions of s 131 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) and that its tender is prevented by that privilege.[28]
The husband says that prior to the mediation, he and the wife signed a Mediation Agreement, which at paragraph 16 read as follows:
The participants agree that subject to Clauses 22 and 23, the following will be privileged and will not be disclosed in, or be the subject of a subpoena to give evidence or to produce documents, in any proceedings in respect of the Dispute:
16.1 any settlement proposal whether made by a party or the mediator;

16.2 the willingness of a party to consider any such proposal;

16.3 any statement made by a party or the mediator during the mediation;

16.4 any information prepared for the mediation that is communicated to another party during the mediation.[29]
In my opinion, the terms of the mediation agreement are clear. They do not permit the adducing of evidence of the course of the mediation or what offers were made in the course of the mediation.
Notwithstanding the terms of the mediation agreement, the wife seeks to rely on a joint balance sheet prepared during that mediation. The husband says that he was not provided with a Balance Sheet or a schedule identifying the effect of the wife’s offer.[30] I reject that contention in circumstances where by his own affidavit:
(a) The husband annexed a letter dated 19 October 2023, which in turn referred to negotiations undertaken at the mediation and necessarily, this must have included the Balance Sheet constructed by the parties;[31]

(b) The husband annexed a further letter dated 13 November 2023, which conveyed an offer but provided no context for the same. Logically, such an offer could only have been made in the context of the mediation in which the parties participated a few weeks prior;[32] and

(c) By letter dated 20 September 2024, the husband’s solicitor referred to an “updated consolidated Balance Sheet”, which responded to an earlier letter annexing the Balance Sheet prepared by the parties at the mediation.[33]

By reference to these factors, I am satisfied that the husband knew of the existence of the Balance Sheet prepared at the mediation and was guided by it in relation to subsequent offers.
As a starting point, s 131 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) (“the Evidence Act”) does not authorise production of the joint balance sheet. Section 131 is in the following terms:
Exclusion of evidence of settlement negotiations

(1) Evidence is not to be adduced of:
(a) a communication that is made between persons in dispute, or between one or more persons in dispute and a third party, in connection with an attempt to negotiate a settlement of the despite; or

(b) a document (whether delivered or not) that has been prepared in connection with an attempt to negotiate a settlement of the dispute.

It is common ground between the parties that the Balance Sheet was a document prepared in connection with a formal mediation in an attempt to resolve their financial dispute. The only question is whether the joint balance sheet is removed from privilege by one of the exemptions referred to at s 131(2) of the Evidence Act.
Although the parties did not address me on the topic, I consider that the only material gateway for the removal of the privilege must be s131(2)(h), which provides that s 131(1) of the Evidence Act does not apply if “the communication or document is relevant to determining liability for costs”. In Silver Fox Co Pty Ltd (as trustee for the Baker Family Trust) & ors v Lenard’s Pty Ltd & ors (No 3) [2004] FCA 1570, Justice Mansfield said as follows:
[33] It may nevertheless be assumed that the terms of the offers made during the mediation are relevant to determining liability for costs, so that prima facie s 131(2)(h) of the Evidence Act would remove the prohibition in s 131(1) from adducing evidence of communications in an attempt to negotiate a settlement of a dispute.
...
[36] Section 131(1) subject to exceptions, gives effect to the policy of ensuring the course of negotiations – whether private or by mediation – are not adduced into evidence for the purpose of influencing the outcome on the primary matters in issue. Clearly, it is in the public interest that negotiations to explore resolution of proceedings should not be inhibited by the risk of such negotiations influencing the outcome on those primary issues. It is equally in the public interest that negotiations should be conducted genuinely and realistically. The effect of s 131(2)(h) is to expose that issue to inspection when costs issues only are to be resolved. There is no apparent public interest in permitting a party to avoid such exposure by imposing terms Husband’s Outline of Case Document filed 20 March 2026, paragraph 5.3.upon the communication, whether by the use of the expression “without prejudice” or by a mediation agreement.
(emphasis added)
However, whether or not the Balance Sheet is “relevant” to determining liability for costs is to be resolved by the application of legal principle and the operation of s 55 of the Evidence Act[34] noting that the operation of this section is not ousted by s 102NL of the Act.
