For the purposes of her application for costs, the wife increased the value attributed to the property at Suburb J from $930,000 to $936,714 being the net sale proceeds realised on 18 December 2023. This being so, the wife says that the only variation to the Joint Balance Sheet prepared at the mediation and the Balance Sheet replicated above is a sum of $6,714.33.[35]
The wife then says as follows:
In a net asset pool (incl. super) of $1,349,801.33, acceptance by [the husband] of the above offer would have resulted in me receiving 52.88% (which is less what I received from the court at 56.65%) and he would have received 47.12% (which is more than what he received from the court at 43.34%).[36]
Adopting a two-pool approach as indeed I did and applying the same overall division of assets, the wife asserts that if her offer dated 17 October 2023 had been accepted by the husband, she would have “walked away” with a sum of $11,654 less than the cash sum ultimately awarded to her.[37]
Similar calculations were made at [7] of the wife’s affidavit filed on 10 March 2026 for the purposes of demonstrating that in effect, the wife was prepared to resolve the competing financial applications for less than the amounts awarded to her pursuant to my Orders made on 12 November 2025.
The wife asserts that in the interests of resolving the competing financial applications at an early stage, she made a number of concessions by her offer dated 17 October 2023. Those concessions included:
(a) An offer to the effect that each party retain his/her own superannuation. That offer stands in distinction to the orders made by me requiring the parties’ superannuation assets to be equalised. The wife says that her offer was significant in circumstances where at the time, the husband had superannuation assets in a sum of $198,231 whilst the wife had superannuation assets of $28,182;[38] and

(b) An offer to include all liabilities including the husband’s credit card liabilities. This again stands in distinction to the orders made by me. Particularly, and at the invitation of the parties, I excluded each party’s liabilities in circumstances where those debts were incurred subsequent to the parties’ separation.[39]

The wife also says that she made a concession by including the Country B properties. I do not agree that the inclusion of these properties represented any concession in circumstances where I found that it was appropriate to include each of the Country B properties in the assets available for distribution.[40] Notwithstanding this, the wife says she is “aggrieved” in circumstances where as a consequence of partial property settlements received during the proceedings,[41] she received the same cash sum as a consequence of the orders made by me as she was prepared to receive in October 2023. The wife says as follows:
What I am particularly aggrieved about is that, as aforementioned, the overall cash I ended up receiving by way of interim and final orders was virtually the same at $499,536 compared to what I offered to walk away with being $500,000. [The husband] ended up receiving cash of $446,518, when I had offered him two years earlier only slightly less at $436,714.[42]
It is worth noting that the form of the orders proposed by the letter dated 17 October 2023 is save and except for the topic of superannuation and the wife’s inclusion of liabilities, consistent with the terms of my final orders. The inclusion of credit card liabilities in fact favoured the husband.
The husband says that it would be imprudent for me to have regard to the Joint Balance Sheet and the effect of the wife’s offer dated 17 October 2023 because to do so would “compare apples with oranges and confused so much more than clarifies” and the task faced by me is “an impossible one”.[43] I do not agree. Indeed, by letter dated 19 October 2023, the husband made an offer in the following terms:
From the net proceeds of the property located at...[Suburb J]; $509,350 be disbursed to the husband and the remainder being $420,650 to the wife.
Any surplus funds from the proceeds of sale will be split equally between the parties.
The wife to retain the properties she owns in [Country B], any cash in her bank accounts, [Motor Vehicle 1] [sic], her shares, superannuation and liabilities.
The husband to retain any cash in his bank accounts, [Motor Vehicle 3], the boat and trailer, [N Platform account], superannuation and liabilities.
Save and except as to the quantum of a cash payment to the wife and the topic of superannuation, the offer:
(a) Reflects the terms of my final orders; and

(b) Reflects the distribution of assets, which I considered at [112] and [113] of my Reasons for Judgment.

This being so, I do not agree with the submission made by the husband’s counsel that the Joint Balance Sheet was “embryonic”.[44] As at 19 October 2023, the husband himself clearly did not consider the Balance Sheet to be “embryonic”. Indeed, it must be the case that by making an offer in the terms described above, the husband considered that the assets had crystallised sufficiently to make an offer. Further, the husband was able to make an offer in circumstances where he was in receipt of valuations in relation to the Country B properties. Those valuations had been jointly requested by the parties pursuant to r 7.03 of the Rules such valuations dated 17 October 2023.[45]
The husband by his counsel’s Outline of Case Document filed on 20 March 2026, asserts that the “real property valuations were disputed” and this being so, explains why he was unable to accept the offer of 17 October 2023. Indeed, on 16 June 2025, the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking to appoint an alternate firm of valuers to provide an opinion as to the value of the properties located in Country B despite the appointment of a single expert in September 2023 (supra). That application was dismissed but on the basis that the husband be at liberty to obtain his own valuation in respect of the properties at his sole expense.[46] On 22 September 2025, being the first day of the final hearing, I declined an application to vacate the trial date so that the husband might obtain a report from his own valuer given his continued dissatisfaction with the opinion expressed by the single expert.
The husband also asserts that the Balance Sheet had not crystallised on account of savings accessed by the wife subsequent to separation in a sum of $39,797.[47] However, I do not consider that such a dispute had any real impact on the ability of the parties to resolve the competing applications in circumstances where:
(a) The parties continued to negotiate on the basis that such monies not be included; and

(b) Even if the amount was included in the Joint Balance Sheet of 17 October 2023, the wife would have still received less than that outcome afforded to her by my Judgment.[48]

For these reasons, I consider that the Balance Sheet referred to by the wife is relevant to her application for costs and I will consider its terms when determining whether it is appropriate to exercise my discretion to make an award of costs in favour of the wife.

FIFTH ISSUE: SHOULD THE COURT EXERCISE ITS DISCRETION TO MAKE AN ORDER FOR COSTS?

Legal Principles

Section 114UB(1) of the Act abolishes for the purposes of family law proceedings, the general rule that in civil proceedings, costs follow the event. It provides that each party should bear his or her own costs in such proceedings.
However, the Court retains a discretion to make an order as to costs. The relevant principles are well settled.[49] The Court has a wide discretion, which is to be exercised judicially.
Relevantly, section 114UB(2) of the Act reads as follows:
If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsection (3) of this section, sections 114UC and 114UD and the applicable rules of Court, make such order as to costs and security for costs, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise.
When considering what order (if any) is just, the legislation mandates that the Court have regard to those matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act. The legislation does not identify that any of those seven matters is determinative. However, the Court must consider each matter in the seven alphabetical subsections.[50] There is nothing to prevent any factor being the sole foundation for any order for costs being made.[51]
Section 114UB(3) of the Act provides that when considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the Court shall have regard to:
(a) the financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings;
(b) whether any party to the proceedings is in receipt of assistance by way of legal aid and, if so, the terms of the grant of that assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer; and

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.
The manner in which costs are to be assessed is dealt with in r 12.17(1) of the Rules, which provides:
(1) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or

(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceeding, or part of an amount, assessed in accordance with Schedule 3.

Rule 12.17(3) of the Rules sets out matters that may be considered in the determination of the quantum of costs, providing that:
(3) In making an order under subrule (1), the court may consider the following:
(a) the importance, complexity or difficulty of the issues;

(b) the reasonableness of each party's behaviour in the proceeding including by having regard to the matters set out in subrule 12.08(2);

(c) the rates ordinarily payable to lawyers in comparable proceedings;

(d) whether a lawyer's conduct has been improper, unfair, unreasonable or disproportionate;

(e) the time properly spent on the proceedings, or in complying with pre-action procedures;

(f) whether expenses (paid or payable) are fair, reasonable and proportionate.

The provisions of the Act and the Rules confer a broad discretion on a judge hearing and determining a costs application.
It is now appropriate to consider whether there are any circumstances which justify a departure from the general rule provided by s 114UB(1) by virtue of any of the considerations specified in s 114UB(3) of the Act.

The financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings

The financial circumstances of each of the parties were outlined at length in my judgment dated 12 November 2025 with respect to the parties’ competing financial applications, namely, Niek & Oddru [2025] FedCFamC1F 802.
The parties’ marriage produced three children, namely, a female child aged 18 years and two male children aged 23 years and 13 years.[52] Pursuant to the terms of orders consented to by the parties on 16 November 2023 and 19 June 2025, the female child lives with the mother and spends time with the father in accordance with her wishes and the youngest male child lives with each of his parents on an equal time basis. Unfortunately, the female child has been diagnosed with an obsessive-compulsive disorder, an attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, oppositional defiance disorder, traits of borderline personality disorder, an eating disorder and body dysmorphia. Her difficulties have been compounded by the use of illicit substances, and her life has been marked by hospitalisations and engagement with police.[53]
The wife has formerly worked in human resources. In March 2025, the wife obtained employment in retail and received a base salary of $95,000 in addition to superannuation.[55] The wife’s employment was based at a “support centre” and she says that this is consistent with the expectations of most employers in the field of human resources who “now require full office presence”.[56]
The wife asserted during the final hearing that the requirements of employers in the human resources area make it extremely difficult for her “to balance the children’s complex needs with the demands of full-time work”.[57] Her carer responsibilities towards the female child and the younger male child were such that in her view, she was “left with little alternative but to resign from my employment”.[58] The eldest male child also had health difficulties and at the time I delivered my Reasons for Judgment he had returned to live with the wife.
The husband earns $3,504 per week on a gross basis in his managerial position in the education sector .[59] It follows that he earns more than double the income previously enjoyed by the wife.
By his affidavit filed on 9 January 2026, the husband advised me that the female child had an altercation with the eldest male child whilst the wife was on holidays overseas with the children. Thereafter, the husband says that the female child began living with him on a full-time basis.[60] The wife says that the husband does not provide the female child with an allowance.[61] Further, it appears that the female child’s living arrangements may be unsettled noting that in February 2026, she sent her mother a message asking her mother to buy her a new bed so that she might recommence sleeping at the wife’s home.[62]
Each of the parties has deposed to what he or she has paid for the benefit of the children in the period subsequent to the delivery of my judgment. However, and by reason of my final orders, the assets now afforded to each party are modest. Axiomatically, each of the parties have been deprived of the item of property best calculated to provide them with future security, namely their own home. Fortunately, the wife retains an interest in two properties in Country B, but I discussed in my Reasons for Judgment the difficulties faced by the wife in realising those interests.
This is not a case in which there is a marked discrepancy in financial resources although the husband does earn a better income. Neither could afford this case, which has been a financial disaster for each of them. The case was folly in financial terms.

Whether any part to the proceedings is receiving assistance by legal aid

Neither party was in receipt of legal aid during these proceedings. Accordingly, this is not a relevant consideration.

The conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings

The wife says that this is a significant factor in the context of this matter. The relevant conduct is the party’s conduct as a litigant.[63]
The wife points to the fact that by his Application in a Proceeding filed on 16 June 2025 and his Application in a Proceeding filed on 19 September 2025, the husband sought to appoint an alternate expert to value the properties located in Country B.
It is correct that the husband’s applications caused the wife to incur expense and it is equally correct that each of the husband’s applications were not successful. However, I do not consider that the mere filing of the applications warrants an order for costs for the period October 2023 to date.
I am more concerned that the husband refused to divide the monies held in the Controlled Monies Account in a manner consistent with my judgment thereby causing each of the parties to incur significant legal fees. The husband’s conduct may reflect the significant animosity, which still exists between the parties but whatever the scenario, common sense dictates that this topic ought to have been capable of resolution between the parties.

Whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the Court

This consideration is not applicable.

Whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings

The husband was wholly unsuccessful with respect to his primary application and was also unsuccessful with respect to his opposition to the division of monies held in the Controlled Money Account (supra).

Whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer

I have carefully analysed the terms of the offer made in writing by the wife on 17 October 2023. By reference to the Balance Sheet prepared during the mediation, which was held on the same day as the offer was made, I consider that the wife’s offer was clear in its terms and capable of being understood. The husband cannot find fault with such a conclusion given that he himself made counteroffers, which could only have been based on the Balance Sheet. I refer to my remarks above in this regard.
I also reject the husband’s assertion that “[one] of the major impediments to [the wife] and I reaching a resolution at mediation was the issues I identified with the valuation reports”.[64] As the wife identifies, the husband could not have been too concerned about the written reports in relation to the value of the Country B properties given that the husband filed no application whatsoever in relation to the topic until 16 June 2025.
Regrettably, and as at 17 October 2023:
(a) The wife had made an offer to “walk away” with a sum of $500,000 and had offered the husband a cash sum of $436,714;[65] and

(b) By letter dated 19 October 2023, the husband offered the wife a cash sum of $420,650 with the husband to receive $509,350.[66]

It follows that the parties were betting on their financial fortunes and for his part, the husband was prepared to continue to final hearing knowing that the difference between the parties two years earlier had only been $79,350.
The wife’s offer was in dollar terms better than the ultimate outcome. The husband would have been better off accepting the offer made on 17 October 2023. I take into account the wife’s offer in considering whether circumstances exist to justify departure from the usual rule.

Conclusion in respect to costs

I am satisfied for the reasons described above that it is in the interests of justice for an order for costs to be made in favour of the wife. The question then becomes the quantum of the order for costs.

CALCULATION OF COSTS

The wife is seeking her costs on a solicitor and client basis as to 75% ($111,470) of the costs she incurred from the time of the offer, which she made on 17 October 2023.
As identified by Justice Riethmuller in Tekla & Tekla [2025] FedCGamCA1 245, the distinction between “party and party” costs and “solicitor and client” costs is between “necessary or proper” and “reasonable”.[67]
In this case, and whilst the husband did not better the wife’s offer, I do not consider that it is appropriate to award the wife anything other than “party and party costs” limited to the fee scale set out in Schedule 3 of the Rules. Particularly, and whilst the husband’s decision to continue with the litigation was regrettable, I do not consider that the wife is entitled to more than the costs “necessary or proper” to attain justice.
The wife invites me to fix a sum equivalent to 75 percent of her total costs.[68] Whilst such an outcome would avoid the uncertainty, additional costs and delay of further proceedings, I am not satisfied that I am able to proceed in the manner sought by the wife.
The wife seeks a sum, which is significant in the circumstances of the parties’ modest assets. Despite this, I was not provided with a short form bill of costs or an itemised schedule of the costs incurred.
For these reasons, I am not able to undertake an assessment of the wife’s costs, which would be “logical, fair and reasonable”.[69]
I will order the husband to pay the wife’s costs of and incidental to the competing financial applications from 17 October 2023 to date on a party and party basis such costs to be agreed but in the absence of agreement as assessed.

APPLICATION FOR COSTS OF THE WIFE’S APPLICATION

The wife also seeks an order that the husband pay her costs of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed on and the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 23 January 2026.
In circumstances where the wife’s applications have been wholly successful, I consider it is appropriate to order the husband to pay the wife’s costs of and incidental to the applications on a party and party basis such costs to be agreed but in the absence of agreement as assessed.

PRESERVATION OF MONIES

As discussed, the wife calculated her costs of the proceedings from 17 October 2023 to be in the order of $111,470. I anticipate that the wife’s costs calculated on a party and party basis will be less than that amount. Nevertheless, and to ensure that the wife receives her party and party costs of the proceedings since 17 October 2023 and otherwise receives her costs of the Application in a Proceeding filed on 10 December 2025 and the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 23 January 2026, I will direct that a sum of $112,000 be preserved in the Trust Account of the husband’s solicitor pending an assessment of the wife’s costs.
